subsystem: libgd / PHP GD image-decoding: gdImageCreateFromXbm(), a parser that reads an X BitMap (XBM) file from a FILE* and constructs an in-memory gdImage. XBM is the C-source image format used by X11 (a text file containing `#define name_width N`, `#define name_height N`, and a `static unsigned char name_bits[] = { 0x.., 0x.., ... };` array). This function is the reachable-from-untrusted-input decoder for that format.
provenance: Identified by the function name gdImageCreateFromXbm and the gdImagePtr/gdImageCreate/gdImageColorAllocate/gdImageSetPixel/gdImageDestroy API, which are the libgd public API. The php_gd_error("invalid XBM") / php_gd_error("EOF before image was complete") calls (lines 108, 125) show this is the PHP bundled copy of libgd (ext/gd). The XBM tokens parsed by sscanf ('#define %s %d', 'static unsigned char %s = {', 'bits[]') are the X BitMap wire format. The header comment /* sample: primevul_c_train_3022_fixed */ marks it as a PrimeVul sample.
trust_boundary: The FILE* fd is entirely attacker-controlled: in PHP this decodes an uploaded/remote image file (e.g. imagecreatefromxbm()). Every byte read via fgets()/getc() is hostile. The attacker fully controls the declared width and height integers (the '#define ... %d' values), the array element type (unsigned char/short selects 8-bit vs 16-bit pixels), the count and content of hex byte tokens, and the total file length. No magic-cookie or size sanity check gates the parse other than the per-line length guard, so the decoder must derive and validate the image geometry itself.

what_the_code_does.explanation: Entry point and local state. fline is the fixed-size line buffer used to read the XBM header text a line at a time; iname holds an identifier token scanned out of a line; both are MAX_XBM_LINE_SIZE bytes (a macro defined elsewhere, not in this file). value receives the parsed integer of a #define. width and height are the declared image dimensions, initialized to 0 so that an absent/zero declaration is detectable. max_bit is 0 until an array-type line is seen, then becomes 128 (8 bits/byte) or 32768 (16 bits/short); it doubles as a 'have we found the pixel array?' flag. bytes is the number of hex tokens expected in the pixel array and, later, the outer loop bound. h[8] is a small scratch buffer into which 2 or 4 ASCII hex characters are copied before sscanf("%x") decodes them into b. x,y,bit,i,ch are pixel/loop cursors.

rewind() resets the stream to the start. The header-parsing loop reads one line at a time with fgets bounded to MAX_XBM_LINE_SIZE, so fline cannot be overrun by fgets itself. Line 21 force-terminates the buffer defensively. Line 22 is a hardening guard: if the line completely filled the buffer (strlen == MAX_XBM_LINE_SIZE-1), the line was too long / possibly truncated, and the function bails out returning 0. This guard is important because it bounds the length of any %s token subsequently scanned out of fline into iname.

Parses '#define <identifier> <int>' lines. The identifier is captured into iname and the integer into value. strrchr(iname,'_') finds the suffix after the last underscore (so 'foo_width' -> 'width'); if there is no underscore the whole name is used. If the suffix is 'width' or 'height', the signed value is cast to unsigned int and stored. Note the value is read as a signed %d and only then reinterpreted as unsigned: a negative literal becomes a very large unsigned dimension. Nothing here bounds value to a sane image size.

For lines that are not #defines, this detects the pixel-array declaration and infers the element width. A 'static [unsigned] char ... = {' declaration means each token is one byte (8 pixels), so max_bit=128 (the highest bit mask, 0x80). A 'static [unsigned] short ... = {' declaration means each token is two bytes (16 pixels), so max_bit=32768 (0x8000). max_bit thus encodes both the bits-per-token and the fact that the array was found. The array identifier is captured into iname.

Once an array declaration has been seen (max_bit != 0), bytes is computed as (width+7)/8 * height — the number of 8-bit tokens for a width-by-height 1bpp bitmap (rows are byte-padded). If that is zero (e.g. width or height still 0 because their #defines have not been seen yet or were 0), the parse aborts. The array identifier's suffix is checked against 'bits[]'; a match means the real pixel array (e.g. 'name_bits[]') was found and the header loop breaks, leaving fd positioned just after the '{'. Note the geometry (bytes) is snapshotted here using whatever width/height are currently known.

After the header loop, both bytes and max_bit must be nonzero or the parse fails. The image is then allocated with gdImageCreate(width, height) using the raw attacker-declared dimensions; on failure the function returns 0. Two palette entries are allocated: index 0 = white (background), index 1 = black (foreground), matching the 1-bit-per-pixel XBM model.

Pre-terminates the hex scratch buffer h. In 8-bit mode only h[0] and h[1] are filled from the stream, so h[2]='\0' makes h a valid 2-char NUL-terminated hex string. In 16-bit mode h[0..3] are filled, so h[4]='\0' terminates a 4-char string. This is the defensive initialization that ensures sscanf(h,"%x") never reads past the bytes actually written — i.e. it prevents an uninitialized/over-read of h.

The pixel loop runs bytes times. For each token it first scans forward with getc until it hits the 'x' of a '0x..' token (or EOF, which sets fail and breaks the outer loop). It then reads the two ASCII hex digits following the 'x' into h[0] and h[1]. Any EOF mid-token breaks out of the loop and is treated as truncation. Each getc result is checked against EOF before being stored, so h[0]/h[1] always receive real bytes when reached.

In 16-bit (short) mode two additional hex digits are read into h[2] and h[3], overwriting the h[2]='\0' terminator set earlier and yielding a 4-char string (h[4] remains '\0'). In 8-bit mode this block is skipped, leaving h[2]='\0' from line 73. This is what keeps the h string correctly terminated for both element sizes.

Decodes the 2- or 4-character hex string into b (unsigned int). If sscanf fails to convert exactly one value (non-hex bytes in the token), the parse is rejected, the partially built image is destroyed, and 0 is returned. b holds the packed pixel bits for this token.

Unpacks the token bit-by-bit (LSB-first: bit mask 1,2,4,...,max_bit) into pixels, plotting color index 1 (black) where the bit is set else 0 (white). x advances per pixel; when x reaches im->sx the row is complete, so x resets, y advances, and the remaining high bits of the token (row padding) are discarded via break. When y reaches im->sy the whole image is filled and im is returned successfully. The x/y cursors are compared against im->sx/im->sy, so the loop's notion of bounds comes from the image object's stored dimensions.

If the byte loop finishes (or breaks on EOF/fail) without y ever reaching im->sy, the file did not contain enough pixel data; an error is logged, the image is destroyed, and 0 is returned. This is the truncated-file path.

what_could_go_wrong.explanation: value is parsed as a signed int with '%d' and then reinterpreted as unsigned with no range check. An attacker can write '#define x_width -1' to make width = 0xFFFFFFFF, or supply enormous positive values. width and height flow unvalidated into both the bytes arithmetic (line 49) and gdImageCreate (line 68). The invariant that must hold is 0 < width,height <= a sane maximum (and width*height must not overflow the allocation size); nothing here enforces it, so the dimensions are the primary hostile primitive.

This computes the token count in unsigned int arithmetic and stores it into a signed int bytes (declared at line 13). With attacker-controlled width/height the multiplication (width+7)/8 * height overflows 32 bits and wraps, and the assignment to a signed int is implementation-defined/UB when the unsigned result exceeds INT_MAX. Effects: (a) bytes can wrap to a small value or to a negative value, decoupling the number of pixel tokens the loop reads (line 75) from the actual image geometry (im->sx*im->sy); (b) width+7 itself overflows when width is near UINT_MAX (e.g. 0xFFFFFFFF+7 -> 6), producing a tiny bytes for a giant image. The guard is only 'if (!bytes) return 0', which rejects zero but not overflow-wrapped or negative values. The invariant is: reject width/height before the multiply so that (width+7)/8*height cannot overflow and bytes is a correct, positive count.

The unvalidated width/height are handed to gdImageCreate (implementation not shown). Historically this libgd allocator multiplies width*height (and allocates per-row pointers plus a sx*sy pixel array); with attacker-chosen giant dimensions this either (a) attempts an enormous allocation (memory-exhaustion DoS) or (b) integer-overflows internally and under-allocates while still recording im->sx=width and im->sy=height. In case (b) the subsequent pixel loop trusts im->sx/im->sy as bounds (lines 114,117) and calls gdImageSetPixel up to sx*sy times against a buffer that is too small — a heap out-of-bounds write. This is a latent finding: the overflow lives in gdImageCreate, but the dangerous contract (passing unbounded dimensions) is created here.

what_could_go_wrong.code:
gdImageSetPixel(im, x++, y, (b & bit) ? 1 : 0);
			if (x == im->sx) {
what_could_go_wrong.explanation: The write loop uses im->sx / im->sy (the values gdImageCreate recorded from the attacker-declared width/height) as its only bounds. If gdImageCreate under-allocated due to a size overflow but still stored the huge sx/sy, gdImageSetPixel will be driven with in-range-looking x,y that exceed the real pixel-buffer allocation, giving a controlled heap overwrite of one palette index per pixel. Even absent an allocator overflow, the loop's correctness depends entirely on bytes*max_bit matching sx*sy; any mismatch (from the line-49 overflow) is masked here because the row/column check, not bytes, decides termination.

This is the CWE-908 (use of uninitialized memory) surface the sample is named for: h[8] is scratch and only h[0..1] (8-bit) or h[0..3] (16-bit) are written from the stream. In THIS version the explicit terminators at lines 73-74 keep h a properly NUL-terminated string in both modes, so sscanf(h,"%x") at line 107 never reads uninitialized bytes h[5..7]. The residual risk to confirm is only that these two terminator assignments are unconditionally executed before the loop (they are) and that no future edit reads h[2]/h[4] in the 16-bit path before they are set — i.e. the mitigation is present but structurally fragile because it depends on the fixed offsets 2 and 4 matching max_bit. This is dismissed-as-mitigated, not an open bug.

The '%s' conversions into iname (here and at lines 39-44) are width-unlimited, which is the classic sscanf buffer-overflow pattern. It is NOT exploitable in this code because iname and fline are both MAX_XBM_LINE_SIZE and line 22 rejects any line whose length reached MAX_XBM_LINE_SIZE-1; therefore a single %s token drawn from fline can never exceed iname's capacity. The invariant to preserve is that iname's size is >= fline's size and the long-line guard stays in place; if either changes, this becomes a stack overflow (CWE-120). Dismissed under current sizing.

summary: Blind review of libgd's gdImageCreateFromXbm XBM decoder. The historic uninitialized-h[] read (CWE-908) that this sample is named for appears mitigated in this version by the explicit terminators at lines 73-74. The material remaining risk is that attacker-declared image dimensions (parsed as signed %d, reinterpreted as unsigned) flow unvalidated into 'bytes = (width+7)/8*height' (integer overflow) and into gdImageCreate(width,height), setting up a latent memory-exhaustion DoS and, if gdImageCreate under-allocates on a size overflow, a heap out-of-bounds write through the gdImageSetPixel loop that trusts im->sx/im->sy.

reviewed_anchors.reason: Public decoder over a fully attacker-controlled FILE*; the trust boundary itself.
Filled only by fgets bounded to MAX_XBM_LINE_SIZE and length-guarded at line 22; no overflow.
Target of unbounded sscanf %s, but bounded in practice by equal buffer size and the line-22 long-line reject.
CWE-908 scratch buffer; only partially written, mitigated by fixed-offset terminators — documented as mitigated.
Attacker-controlled dimensions, cast from signed %d, unvalidated; drive allocation and geometry.
Signed parse of a dimension later cast to unsigned int; negative value becomes huge dimension.
Selects 8- vs 16-bit token width; constant, not attacker-sizable beyond these two values; bounds the h reads correctly.
Unsigned-arithmetic multiply stored into signed int; overflow/wrap, only guarded against zero.
Receives unvalidated dimensions; latent allocation overflow/DoS in implementation not shown.
Write bound comes from im->sx/im->sy; OOB write if allocation and geometry disagree.
Unbounded %s but bounded by buffer sizing and the long-line guard.
Same %s bound as above; only sets max_bit; no overflow.
Reads NUL-terminated h; return value checked; b only used when conversion succeeds.
Defensive control that makes the %s scans safe; not itself a bug.
Not defined in this file; sizing of fline/iname; assumed a fixed positive constant.
Pointer into NUL-terminated iname; no OOB; only classification.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: for (i = 0; i < bytes; i++) pixel loop (line 75)
reviewed_anchors.reason: Loop bound is the possibly-overflowed signed bytes; ties to the line-49 overflow.
Every getc result checked against EOF before store; no uninitialized store, no overflow of h.

findings.title: Unvalidated attacker-controlled XBM dimensions flow to gdImageCreate — integer overflow and latent heap out-of-bounds write
findings.anchor: width = (unsigned int) value; / gdImageCreate(width, height) / gdImageSetPixel(im, x++, y, ...)
findings.bug_class: Integer overflow of image dimensions leading to under-allocation and heap out-of-bounds write
findings.where_it_lives: implementation (file not provided) — gdImageCreate; write manifests at gdImageSetPixel line 113 in this file
findings.invariant: width and height must be validated to be strictly positive and within a sane maximum, and width*height (plus per-row overhead) must be proven not to overflow the allocation size, BEFORE calling gdImageCreate; the pixel loop's bounds (im->sx/im->sy) must equal the actually-allocated buffer geometry.
findings.failure_mode: An XBM with '#define x_width -1' (or otherwise huge width/height) makes width/height ~0xFFFFFFFF. gdImageCreate either attempts a giant allocation (memory-exhaustion DoS) or internally overflows width*height and under-allocates while still storing im->sx=width, im->sy=height. The pixel loop then drives gdImageSetPixel using im->sx/im->sy as bounds (lines 114,117), writing one palette index per pixel past the real allocation — a controlled heap overflow reachable from imagecreatefromxbm() on an untrusted file.
findings.what_to_confirm: Whether gdImageCreate bounds width/height and uses overflow-checked size arithmetic (overflow2/gdMalloc guards) for the row-pointer array and the sx*sy pixel buffer, and whether it fails safely for dimensions near UINT_MAX.

findings.title: bytes token count computed with overflow-prone unsigned arithmetic stored into signed int
findings.bug_class: Integer overflow / signedness (unsigned result truncated into signed int)
findings.invariant: width and height must be range-checked before this multiply so that (width+7) cannot wrap, (width+7)/8*height cannot exceed INT_MAX, and the resulting positive bytes correctly equals the number of hex tokens for a width x height 1bpp bitmap.
findings.failure_mode: With attacker-chosen dimensions the computation overflows 32-bit unsigned (e.g. width=0xFFFFFFFF makes width+7 wrap to 6, so bytes becomes tiny) and the assignment into signed int bytes is UB/implementation-defined when the result exceeds INT_MAX (can become negative or a small value). The only guard is 'if (!bytes) return 0', which catches exactly zero but not wrapped/negative results, so the token loop bound (line 75) no longer matches the true image size — feeding the geometry/allocation mismatch in the primary finding.
findings.what_to_confirm: That width/height are validated before line 49 and that bytes is computed in a wide unsigned type with overflow checks, or that gdImageCreate's own validation makes an inconsistent bytes harmless.

findings.title: Uninitialized hex scratch buffer h[] — CWE-908 surface, mitigated in this revision by fixed-offset terminators
findings.bug_class: Use of uninitialized/over-read memory in string parsing
findings.invariant: h must be NUL-terminated at the correct offset for the active element width (offset 2 for 8-bit, offset 4 for 16-bit) before every sscanf(h, "%x") so the conversion never reads bytes that were not written from the stream.
findings.failure_mode: If the terminators at lines 73-74 were removed or made conditional (as in the vulnerable sibling of this sample), sscanf(h,"%x") in 8-bit mode would scan past h[1] into uninitialized stack bytes h[2..7], producing nondeterministic pixel values / information exposure. In this fixed version the two unconditional terminator writes precede the loop and cover both modes, so no uninitialized read occurs; the finding is recorded as mitigated because the safety depends on the literal offsets 2 and 4 tracking max_bit rather than on a computed length.
findings.what_to_confirm: That lines 73-74 always execute before the read loop on every path and that no code path in the 16-bit branch reads h[2]/h[3] before writing them.

audit_checklist: Verify gdImageCreate validates width/height (>0 and <= a hard maximum) and uses overflow-checked allocation for both the row-pointer array and the sx*sy pixel buffer; confirm behavior for dimensions near UINT_MAX.
Confirm width/height are bounded before line 49 so (width+7)/8*height cannot overflow and bytes is a correct positive int.
Reject negative/zero '#define *_width'/'*_height' values at parse time (line 33/36) instead of casting a signed value straight to unsigned.
Confirm the pixel-write loop's bounds (im->sx/im->sy) always equal the actually-allocated buffer geometry so gdImageSetPixel cannot write OOB.
Confirm h[2]='\0' and h[4]='\0' (lines 73-74) run unconditionally before the read loop and that the 16-bit path never reads h[2]/h[3] before writing them.
Confirm MAX_XBM_LINE_SIZE keeps fline and iname equal-sized and the line-22 long-line reject stays, so the unbounded sscanf %s conversions cannot overflow iname.
